subtopic_detail: Lichen sclerosus diagnosis and ultrapotent topical corticosteroid induction
stem: You are the GP registrar reviewing a 58-year-old postmenopausal woman with 6 months of vulval itch, soreness and dyspareunia. She has tried over-the-counter topical clotrimazole without benefit. There is no urinary or bowel symptom, and she has not noticed unusual discharge. She has a personal history of autoimmune thyroiditis. Examination shows the typical figure-of-eight white atrophic plaques involving the labia minora, clitoris and perianal region with loss of architecture and partial fusion of the clitoral hood. There are no ulcers or focal masses. The vagina is not involved.

data_table.Examination: Symmetrical figure-of-eight white atrophic plaques, loss of labia minora architecture, partial clitoral hood fusion, no ulceration
data_table.Investigations: No prior biopsy; STI swabs negative; vaginal pH and discharge normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Prescribe topical oestrogen cream nightly for 12 weeks because postmenopausal vulval atrophy is the most likely diagnosis and topical oestrogen restores vulval epithelial integrity and improves dyspareunia and itch
options.rationale: The architectural change with loss of labia minora and clitoral hood fusion is characteristic of lichen sclerosus rather than atrophic vulvovaginitis, and topical oestrogen is not the first-line treatment. (Source: Australasian College of Dermatologists: Lichen sclerosus)
source_refs: Australasian College of Dermatologists: Lichen sclerosus

options.text: Commence clobetasol propionate 0.05% ointment nightly for 4 weeks, then alternate nights for 4 weeks, then twice weekly maintenance, with safety-net advice to return if any ulcer, fissure or focal mass develops
options.rationale: Ultrapotent topical corticosteroid induction-then-taper-then-maintenance is standard for classic LS; biopsy is reserved for atypical lesions, treatment failure, ulceration or focal masses. (Source: Australasian College of Dermatologists: Lichen sclerosus)
source_refs: Australasian College of Dermatologists: Lichen sclerosus

options.text: Refer urgently to gynaecology oncology for biopsy under general anaesthesia and consideration of wide local excision because of the multifocal white plaques in a postmenopausal woman that may represent VIN or invasive squamous cell carcinoma
options.rationale: Typical LS without ulceration, induration, focal mass or persistence despite therapy does not require urgent oncology referral or biopsy; ongoing surveillance is enough. (Source: Australasian College of Dermatologists: Lichen sclerosus)
source_refs: Australasian College of Dermatologists: Lichen sclerosus

options.text: Prescribe a 6-week course of topical hydrocortisone 1% twice daily and a vulval emollient and reassess at 6 weeks, reserving stronger topical steroids for refractory cases that have failed milder therapy
options.rationale: Mild-potency corticosteroids are insufficient for LS; under-treatment risks ongoing inflammation, scarring and progression. Ultrapotent steroids are recommended for induction. (Source: Australasian College of Dermatologists: Lichen sclerosus)
source_refs: Australasian College of Dermatologists: Lichen sclerosus

options.text: Treat empirically with oral fluconazole 150 mg once weekly for 6 weeks for presumed recurrent vulvovaginal candidiasis given the unresponsiveness to topical clotrimazole and consider HRT systemic therapy
options.rationale: Recurrent candidiasis does not produce architectural change; the clinical picture is LS, not candidiasis, and antifungals will not help. (Source: Australasian College of Dermatologists: Lichen sclerosus)
source_refs: Australasian College of Dermatologists: Lichen sclerosus

explanation.summary: Vulval lichen sclerosus is a chronic inflammatory dermatosis with bimodal age distribution (prepubertal girls and postmenopausal women). Classical findings are figure-of-eight pale atrophic plaques sparing the vagina, with architectural change (clitoral hood fusion, labial resorption, posterior fourchette fissuring). Diagnosis is clinical for typical cases; biopsy is reserved for atypical features, treatment failure, focal mass or ulceration. First-line treatment is ultrapotent topical corticosteroid (clobetasol 0.05% ointment) nightly induction, then taper to twice-weekly maintenance, with annual surveillance for differentiated VIN and vulval SCC (lifetime risk around 4 to 5%).
key_points: Bimodal age: prepubertal girls and postmenopausal women
Typical: figure-of-eight white plaques, architectural change, vagina spared
First-line: clobetasol 0.05% ointment OD induction, then twice-weekly maintenance
Biopsy if atypical features, treatment failure, focal mass or ulceration
Lifetime SCC risk 4-5%: annual surveillance lifelong
explanation.pearls: Painful fissures at the posterior fourchette in a postmenopausal woman with dyspareunia is a classical LS hook; lubricant alone is a routine misstep that misses the diagnosis and lets architectural change progress to permanent introital narrowing.

sources.label: Australasian College of Dermatologists: Lichen sclerosus
BAD Clinical Guideline: Vulval lichen sclerosus

subtopic_detail: First-line management of stress urinary incontinence with supervised pelvic floor training
stem: You are the gynaecology registrar in clinic seeing a 48-year-old G3P3 with 3 years of involuntary urine loss with cough, sneeze and lifting her grandchildren. She does not get up at night to void and denies urgency, frequency, dysuria or urinary tract infections. Her BMI is 31. She is still menstruating regularly and is not on any hormonal medication. She drinks 4 cups of coffee daily. A 3-day bladder diary shows average daily fluid intake of 2.5 L, 7 voids per day, no nocturia and 4 episodes of stress leakage with provocation. Postvoid residual on bladder scan is 35 mL. Urinalysis is unremarkable. Cough stress test reveals immediate leakage with full bladder and no urethral hypermobility on examination.

data_table.Examination: Cough stress test positive at full bladder, no demonstrable cystocele or rectocele, no urethral hypermobility, well-supported anterior vaginal wall
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis nil; PVR 35 mL; 3-day diary as above

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line management?

options.text: Refer for urodynamic studies to confirm urodynamic stress incontinence before commencing any conservative therapy because the bladder diary findings are subjective and may not distinguish stress from mixed incontinence
options.rationale: Urodynamics are reserved for failed conservative therapy or for mixed/atypical pictures, not first-line for clinically obvious pure SUI. (Source: RACGP: Urinary incontinence in women)

options.text: Refer for placement of a mid-urethral retropubic mesh sling as definitive first-line surgical therapy given her relatively young age and the impact on her quality of life and ability to engage with her grandchildren
options.rationale: Surgical mid-urethral sling is reserved for failed adequate conservative therapy in Australian practice; supervised pelvic floor training is first-line. (Source: RACGP: Urinary incontinence in women)

options.text: Trial oxybutynin 5 mg twice daily for 6 weeks and review symptoms with a repeat 3-day bladder diary before considering escalation to mirabegron or referral for urodynamic assessment and surgical treatment
options.rationale: Antimuscarinics treat urgency or urge incontinence, not stress incontinence; this patient has no urgency component. (Source: RACGP: Urinary incontinence in women)

options.text: Refer for supervised pelvic floor muscle training with a continence physiotherapist for 12 weeks, advise weight loss aiming for 5 to 10% body weight reduction and reduce caffeine intake from 4 to under 2 cups daily
options.rationale: Supervised pelvic floor training (8-12 weeks) plus lifestyle modification (weight loss, caffeine reduction) is the recommended first-line for SUI in Australia; surgical and pharmacological options are second-line. (Source: RACGP: Urinary incontinence in women)

options.text: Commence intravaginal oestrogen pessary nightly for 6 weeks and review, on the basis that oestrogen deficiency contributes to urethral closure failure even in still-menstruating women over 40 years of age
options.rationale: Topical oestrogen is helpful for postmenopausal urogenital atrophy contributing to incontinence but is not first-line in a still-menstruating premenopausal woman with classical SUI. (Source: RACGP: Urinary incontinence in women)

explanation.summary: Stress urinary incontinence in women is initially managed conservatively with supervised pelvic floor muscle training (8 to 12 weeks of at least three sets of eight to twelve contractions daily under physiotherapist guidance), lifestyle modification (weight loss if BMI over 25, reduction in caffeine and bladder irritants, smoking cessation), and bladder training. Urodynamics are reserved for failed conservative therapy, mixed picture or planning surgery. Mid-urethral sling is the standard surgical option after failure of conservative measures; recent Australian regulatory restrictions on mesh have shifted toward autologous fascial sling and Burch colposuspension in some women.
key_points: First-line SUI: supervised PFMT 8-12 weeks + lifestyle (weight, caffeine, smoking)
Antimuscarinics treat urgency, not SUI - check diary for symptom phenotype
Topical oestrogen for postmenopausal urogenital atrophy contribution
Urodynamics: failed conservative therapy or planning surgery
Surgery: mid-urethral sling, autologous fascial sling, Burch colposuspension
explanation.pearls: Bladder diary with paired symptom triggers is the most cost-effective single test - it distinguishes pure stress from urgency-predominant or mixed, separates polydipsia-driven volume from low-volume incontinence, and dictates whether pelvic floor training or antimuscarinics are appropriate.

sources.label: Continence Foundation of Australia: Clinical guidelines

subtopic_detail: Recognition of absolute contraindications to systemic menopausal hormone therapy
stem: You are the GP registrar reviewing a 52-year-old woman with 18 months of vasomotor symptoms (10 to 12 hot flushes per day, severe night sweats waking her 4 times nightly), mood changes and sleep disturbance. Her last menstrual period was 14 months ago. She has tried cognitive behavioural therapy for hot flushes, paroxetine 20 mg daily for 12 weeks (no benefit, ceased 6 weeks ago) and clonidine 50 mcg twice daily (no benefit). She had a unilateral hormone-receptor-positive invasive ductal breast carcinoma 6 years ago treated with lumpectomy, sentinel lymph node biopsy, radiotherapy and 5 years of tamoxifen, completed 12 months ago. She has no evidence of recurrence and was discharged from oncology surveillance 6 months ago.

data_table.Examination: Unremarkable; both breasts soft with surgical scar on right; no axillary lymphadenopathy
data_table.Investigations: Last surveillance mammogram 6 months ago: normal; FSH not measured (clinically postmenopausal)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management of her vasomotor symptoms?

options.text: Commence transdermal oestradiol 50 micrograms patch twice weekly with cyclical micronised progesterone 200 mg orally nightly for 12 days each month and review symptom response at 12 weeks
options.rationale: Prior hormone-receptor-positive breast cancer is a contraindication to systemic menopausal hormone therapy regardless of time since treatment. (Source: Australasian Menopause Society: MHT position statement)
source_refs: Australasian Menopause Society: MHT position statement

options.text: Prescribe tibolone 2.5 mg daily because it has a lower breast cancer risk profile than combined oestrogen-progestogen and has been shown to be safe to use in breast cancer survivors with severe vasomotor symptoms
options.rationale: Tibolone is associated with increased breast cancer recurrence (LIBERATE trial) and is contraindicated in breast cancer survivors. (Source: Australasian Menopause Society: MHT position statement)
source_refs: Australasian Menopause Society: MHT position statement

options.text: Trial venlafaxine 37.5 mg daily titrated to 75 mg daily over 2 weeks for vasomotor symptoms and refer her to a sleep psychologist for CBT-insomnia given the persistent night sweats and sleep disturbance
options.rationale: Venlafaxine is an effective non-hormonal option for VMS in women in whom MHT is contraindicated; SSRIs/SNRIs and gabapentin are recommended in breast cancer survivors. (Source: Australasian Menopause Society: MHT position statement)
source_refs: Australasian Menopause Society: MHT position statement

options.text: Refer to a specialist menopause clinic for consideration of low-dose vaginal oestrogen as systemic treatment for hot flushes because the systemic absorption from vaginal preparations is too low to affect breast cancer recurrence risk
options.rationale: Vaginal oestrogen treats urogenital atrophy, not systemic vasomotor symptoms, and is best avoided in breast cancer survivors during active aromatase inhibitor therapy; not the answer for VMS. (Source: Australasian Menopause Society: MHT position statement)
source_refs: Australasian Menopause Society: MHT position statement

options.text: Recommend phytoestrogens (red clover, black cohosh) at over-the-counter doses with a focus on lifestyle modification including avoiding triggers, layered clothing, weight maintenance and regular exercise as first-line for breast cancer survivors
options.rationale: Phytoestrogen efficacy is at best equivalent to placebo for VMS and they have not been shown safe long-term in oestrogen-receptor-positive breast cancer survivors. (Source: Australasian Menopause Society: MHT position statement)
source_refs: Australasian Menopause Society: MHT position statement

explanation.summary: Absolute contraindications to systemic menopausal hormone therapy include current or prior breast cancer, current or prior oestrogen-dependent cancer (e.g. some endometrial cancers), undiagnosed vaginal bleeding, active VTE or known thrombophilia with prior thrombosis, active liver disease, untreated severe hypertension and known cardiovascular disease. Non-hormonal options for VMS include SSRIs (paroxetine, escitalopram), SNRIs (venlafaxine, desvenlafaxine), gabapentin, pregabalin, oxybutynin and CBT. Tibolone is contraindicated in breast cancer survivors. Vaginal oestrogen for urogenital atrophy in breast cancer survivors is generally avoided during active aromatase inhibitor therapy; it can be considered after MDT discussion otherwise.
key_points: Absolute MHT contraindications: prior breast/oestrogen-dependent cancer, active VTE, severe liver disease
Non-hormonal VMS: SSRI/SNRI (venlafaxine, escitalopram), gabapentin, oxybutynin
Paroxetine + tamoxifen: avoid (CYP2D6 interaction reduces tamoxifen efficacy)
Tibolone contraindicated in breast cancer survivors (LIBERATE)
Vaginal oestrogen for urogenital atrophy: avoid with aromatase inhibitors
explanation.pearls: Paroxetine and fluoxetine inhibit CYP2D6 and reduce conversion of tamoxifen to its active metabolite endoxifen; in a tamoxifen-taking patient, switch to venlafaxine or escitalopram instead of paroxetine for VMS or depression.

sources.label: Australasian Menopause Society: MHT position statement
Cancer Australia: Menopausal hormone therapy after breast cancer

subtopic_detail: Clozapine red-result neutrophil threshold and immediate action
stem: You are the psychiatry registrar on a community clozapine clinic. A 34-year-old man with treatment-resistant schizophrenia has been on clozapine 400 mg daily for 18 months with full symptom remission. His weekly FBC for the first 18 weeks was normal; after the first 6 months he was on 4-weekly monitoring and his most recent FBC 5 weeks ago was a green result. Today's FBC, taken because of 2 days of sore throat and rigors, shows WBC 2.1 x10^9/L (was 6.8 x10^9/L last month), absolute neutrophil count 1.1 x10^9/L (was 4.2 x10^9/L last month), platelets 240 x10^9/L, Hb 142 g/L. He is afebrile (37.8 °C) but well in himself with no localising signs of infection.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.8 °C, BP 120/76 mmHg, HR 88, SpO2 98% room air, RR 18
data_table.Examination: Mild pharyngeal erythema, no exudate or tonsillar swelling, no cervical adenopathy, chest clear, no other findings
data_table.Investigations: WBC 2.1, ANC 1.1, plt 240, Hb 142, CRP 18; viral PCR pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate action?

options.text: Continue clozapine at current dose, repeat FBC in 24 hours and reassure that an isolated mild drop is common with viral illness in the first 18 months of treatment and resolves spontaneously without dose change
options.rationale: ANC of 1.1 x10^9/L is an amber result requiring twice-weekly monitoring and immediate review; clinical assumption of viral cause does not exempt from protocol. (Source: RANZCP: Clozapine treatment recommendations)

options.text: Halve clozapine to 200 mg daily as a precautionary dose reduction, repeat FBC in 7 days and review at the next routine clinic appointment after the protocol-required interval has elapsed for medication adjustment review
options.rationale: Reducing dose without proper protocol does not address agranulocytosis risk; the Australian clozapine monitoring system uses specific action thresholds, not arbitrary dose halving. (Source: RANZCP: Clozapine treatment recommendations)

options.text: Cease clozapine, admit to a medical ward for haematology consultation, take blood cultures, commence broad-spectrum IV antibiotics empirically and consider granulocyte colony-stimulating factor if ANC falls below 0.5 x10^9/L
options.rationale: This is the appropriate action for a red result (ANC under 1.0 x10^9/L) or for clinical sepsis; the current result is amber and not yet red. (Source: RANZCP: Clozapine treatment recommendations)

options.text: Switch to olanzapine 20 mg daily today and arrange weekly FBC for 8 weeks to ensure recovery of haematological parameters before considering rechallenge with clozapine or alternative antipsychotic options
options.rationale: Switching antipsychotic on a single amber FBC bypasses the recommended monitoring escalation; abrupt cessation of clozapine also risks rebound psychosis. (Source: RANZCP: Clozapine treatment recommendations)

options.text: Continue clozapine at current dose, escalate to twice-weekly FBC monitoring until two consecutive green results, review for infection (chest X-ray, blood cultures if febrile or focal signs), and notify the clozapine coordinator and prescribing psychiatrist
options.rationale: ANC 1.0 to 1.5 x10^9/L (or WBC 2.0 to 3.0) is an amber result: continue clozapine, monitor twice weekly, review for infection and notify the clozapine coordinator. Stop only if ANC under 1.0 or there are clinical features of sepsis. (Source: RANZCP: Clozapine treatment recommendations)

explanation.summary: Clozapine carries a 1 to 2% lifetime risk of agranulocytosis and 3% of neutropenia. The Australian monitoring system uses traffic-light thresholds (specific thresholds may vary by manufacturer but a common scheme is): green WBC at least 3.5 and ANC at least 2.0, amber WBC 3.0 to 3.5 or ANC 1.5 to 2.0 (continue with weekly FBC) or WBC 2.0 to 3.0 or ANC 1.0 to 1.5 (continue with twice-weekly FBC, look for infection), red WBC under 2.0 or ANC under 1.0 (stop clozapine immediately, admit, haematology referral, sepsis workup, consider G-CSF). After 18 weeks monitoring drops to monthly, but any abnormal result reverts to weekly or more frequent.
key_points: Clozapine agranulocytosis risk 1-2% lifetime; highest in first 6 months
Green: WBC at least 3.5 AND ANC at least 2.0
Amber WBC 2.0-3.0 or ANC 1.0-1.5: continue, twice-weekly FBC
Red WBC under 2.0 or ANC under 1.0: STOP, admit, haematology, sepsis workup
After 18 weeks: monthly FBC, but abnormal reverts to weekly+
explanation.pearls: The lifetime clozapine rechallenge after stopping for haematological reasons is generally only permitted via the manufacturer's special-access scheme and after haematology and psychiatric MDT review; do not casually restart clozapine after a red result.

subtopic_detail: Lithium long-term monitoring schedule for renal, thyroid and calcium function
stem: You are the psychiatry registrar in a community mental health clinic seeing a 45-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder maintained on lithium carbonate 1000 mg nocte for 8 years. She has been euthymic on this dose throughout. Her last set of routine bloods 6 months ago showed lithium level 0.7 mmol/L (target 0.6 to 0.8 mmol/L for maintenance), eGFR 78 mL/min/1.73m^2, TSH 3.4 mIU/L, corrected calcium 2.42 mmol/L. She drinks 1.5 to 2 litres of water daily, no NSAID use, no recent dehydration, no new medications. She has no current symptoms of toxicity.

data_table.Examination: No tremor, normal reflexes, no goitre, no signs of dehydration
data_table.Investigations: Last set 6 months ago as above

lead_in: What is the recommended routine monitoring schedule for her stable maintenance lithium therapy?

options.text: Lithium level every 6 months together with UEC, TFTs and corrected calcium every 6 months; review for new medications (NSAIDs, ACE-inhibitors, diuretics) and dehydration at every appointment
options.rationale: RANZCP and AMH guidance is 6-monthly lithium level plus 6-monthly UEC, TFTs and corrected calcium in stable maintenance lithium beyond the first year. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Mood disorders (bipolar))

options.text: Lithium level monthly, UEC monthly, TFTs annually, corrected calcium annually; review for drug interactions at every visit because of the narrow therapeutic index of lithium therapy in older adults
options.rationale: Monthly lithium and UEC is unnecessarily frequent for a stable patient and not recommended; this schedule applies to initiation or dose change. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Mood disorders (bipolar))

options.text: Lithium level annually, UEC annually, TFTs annually, corrected calcium 2-yearly; counsel on hydration and to report tremor, nausea or polyuria immediately as warning signs of impending lithium toxicity
options.rationale: Annual monitoring intervals are too long; 6-monthly is the recommended minimum for stable maintenance. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Mood disorders (bipolar))

options.text: Lithium level every 3 months, UEC every 3 months, TFTs every 6 months, corrected calcium annually; review medication interactions, dehydration risk and lithium toxicity signs at every visit
options.rationale: Three-monthly lithium and UEC is more frequent than required for stable patients and not consistent with Australian guidance. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Mood disorders (bipolar))

options.text: Lithium level 4-weekly until 6 months from any dose change then 6-monthly thereafter, TFTs and UEC only if clinically symptomatic, corrected calcium not routinely required for long-term lithium
options.rationale: Routine TFTs, UEC and corrected calcium are required, not only if symptomatic, because hypothyroidism and hyperparathyroidism can be insidious. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Mood disorders (bipolar))

explanation.summary: Long-term lithium therapy requires monitoring for therapeutic level (narrow therapeutic index 0.4 to 1.0 mmol/L), nephrotoxicity (chronic interstitial nephritis, nephrogenic diabetes insipidus), hypothyroidism (around 10 to 20% over years), and hyperparathyroidism with hypercalcaemia. The recommended monitoring for stable maintenance therapy is 6-monthly lithium level, UEC, TFTs and corrected calcium. Initiation or dose change triggers more frequent monitoring (5 to 7 days after dose change). Drug interactions to flag are NSAIDs, ACE-inhibitors, ARBs, thiazides (all raise lithium), and dehydration (volume contraction raises lithium). Levels are measured 12 hours post-dose (trough).
key_points: Stable maintenance lithium: 6-monthly level, UEC, TFTs, corrected calcium
Therapeutic range: 0.6-0.8 maintenance, 0.8-1.0 acute
Level taken 12 hours post-dose (trough)
Watch interactions: NSAIDs, ACEI/ARBs, thiazides all raise lithium
Long-term risks: CKD, hypothyroidism, hyperparathyroidism, nephrogenic DI
explanation.pearls: If a patient on chronic lithium presents with polyuria and polydipsia, do not assume diabetes mellitus; a urine osmolality below serum osmolality despite hyperosmolar plasma confirms nephrogenic diabetes insipidus and may persist after lithium cessation.
